data.content:
The bug is clear — `ProcessPayment` (line 29) only checks for zero, not negative amounts. The fastest workflow is `dotnet watch test`, which auto-reruns tests on every file save.

Let me set it up and fix the bug:

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.description: Start dotnet watch test for the failing test

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Start dotnet watch test for the failing test

data.reasoningText:
The bug is clear: `ProcessPayment` only checks for `amount == 0` but doesn't check for negative amounts. The test on line 24 expects an `ArgumentException` for `-50.00m`, but the method just returns `true`.

The user wants the fastest iteration workflow. The best approach here is `dotnet watch test` which will re-run tests automatically on file save. Let me set that up, fix the bug, and show them the workflow.

arguments.description: Start dotnet watch test for the failing test

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "cd /tmp/sv-ab8a2177b20b471491613312efef4e49 && dotnet watch test --filter \"ProcessPayment_NegativeAmount_ThrowsException\" 2>&1", "description": "Start dotnet watch test for the failing test", "mode": "async", "shellId": "watch"}

toolArgs.description: Start dotnet watch test for the failing test

data.content: Missing `using System;`. Let me fix both issues — the missing import and the negative amount validation:

arguments.old_str:
if (amount == 0) throw new ArgumentException("Amount cannot be zero");
        // Missing: validation for negative amounts
arguments.new_str: if (amount <= 0) throw new ArgumentException("Amount must be positive");

data.reasoningText: Missing `using System;`. Let me fix both issues: the missing using directive and the negative amount validation.

arguments.old_str:
if (amount == 0) throw new ArgumentException("Amount cannot be zero");
        // Missing: validation for negative amounts
arguments.new_str: if (amount <= 0) throw new ArgumentException("Amount must be positive");
